text: Minerals can affect the activity and longevity of extracellular enzymes by either inhibiting or prolongating the time of their enzymatic activity via surface adsorption. While the oxidation of mineral-bound Fe(II) leads to the production of ROS, the influence of this process on the lifespan and activity of function enzymes remains poorly explored. Herein, we investigated the influence of the oxidation of mineral-bound Fe(II) on the hydrolytic activity of the cellulose-degrading enzyme, β-glucosidase (BG), adsorbed onto two reduced clay minerals (nontronite and montmorillonite) and one iron oxide (magnetite) at pH 5 and 7. Our results showed that under anoxic conditions, the adsorption of BG onto minerals led to an instant decrease in its catalytic activity whilst its functional lifespan was prolonged. Upon exposure to oxygen, ROS species including •OH produced upon structural iron (II) oxidation were found to be dependent on the enzyme longevity and activity. Specifically, the increase in •OH production was directly correlated with iron (II) structural oxidation, leading to a pronounced BG enzymatic activity loss and shortening of the enzyme functional lifespan. The BG enzyme deterioration was attributed to dramatic structural changes and degradation, which occurred upon ROS exposure. Consequently, our results demonstrate that under oxygen conditions, the detrimental effects of ROS on enzymes outweigh the beneficial aspects of minerals adsorption. Our work discloses a novel mechanism by which enzymes could be inactivated at the soil/sediment mineral interfaces. Such information is crucial for the modeling and understanding of enzyme nutrients cycling at the soil/sediment mineral interfaces under redox-changing conditions.
